1: <header>Effects of residence time on life cycle assessment of bioenergy production from dairy manure </header>
Raja Chowdhury, Jhuma Sadhukhan Marzia Traversoc, Patricia L. Keen

<list>a Dept. of Civil Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Roorkee, India 
b Center of Environmental Strategy, University of Surrey, Guildford, UK 
C Institute of Sustainability in Civil Engineering, RWTH Aachen University, Germany 
d Dept. of Civil Engineering, University of British Columbia, Canada</list>

ARTICLE INFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Algal bioenergy Life cycle assessment Energy demand analysis GHG emissions|This study simulated the effect of time on bioenergy production from dairy manure and associated variation in energy demand and GHG emission in Ohio and Hawaii. Increasing the residence-time (RT) decreases the bioenergy-production in a nonlinear-fashion, for both the states (Ohio and Hawaii). Using the main scenario in Hawaii, the highest bioenergy production for 30 days RT was 11.2 104 GJ. Life-cycle-assessment of produced bioenergy showed that energy requirement and GHG emission of the produced-bioenergy (GJ) varied from 0.65 to 0.67 and 28-35 kg CO2/GJ bioenergy respectively. Year-round bioenergy production through main scenario was more advantageous with respect to bioenergy production and GHG emission. Decreasing nitrogen con- centration for algal biomass production increased the bioenergy production significantly with a reduced energy demand and marginally increased GHG emission. Hence, the LCA model predicted that running a biorefinery for short residence time, and using diluted waste, could provide bioenergy with reduced energy requirement and GHG-emission.
2: Waste-based biorefinery in which waste materials would be used to produce valuable products and bioenergy have gained popularity re- cently. The primary advantage of the waste biorefinery, which converts waste to products is economically advantageous as compared to pro- ducing the same products from other conventional materials. In addi- tion to economic advantages, uses of waste materials for producing value-added products can reduce the environmental impacts from waste materials (Singh et al., 2010). In this study, we focused on dairy manure-based bioenergy production through an algal route. Dairy waste, (sometimes referred to as dairy manure), has been demonstrated to contain a considerable fraction of nutrients, and numerous phar- maceutical products (Boxall et al., 2003; Wohde et al., 2016). As esti- mated, dairy operation in the USA produces 6 million tons of nitrogen and 1 million ton of phosphorus as manure (Chowdhury and Freire, 2015). Recently, the scientific community has expressed their great concern for depleting resources of phosphorus, and mentioned that, at the current rate of consumption of phosphorus, the reserve will be ex- hausted within the next 30 years (Cordell et al., 2009). As phosphorus is an essential part of any living organisms, it is required for food

production. Hence, producing bioenergy from inorganic nutrients in a considerable amount would not be possible (Batan et al., 2010). Therefore, using dairy manure for bioenergy production can reduce the depletion of the limited phosphorus reserve, and reduce the problem of contamination of groundwater and surface water from contaminants present in dairy manure used as a soil amendment in business as usual case. Several studies showed that algae grow vigorously during the treatment of raw or anaerobically digested dairy waste or other high strength waste (Wang et al., 2010; He et al., 2013; Debowski et al., 2017). In earlier studies, the present authors have estimated biofuel production potential using dairy manure produced in the US (Chowdhury and Freire, 2015; Chowdhury and Franchetti, 2017). For bioenergy production, in addition to anaerobic digestion, algal biomass production using effluent from anaerobic digester was considered. Algal biomass was further processed for producing various forms of bioe- nergy. To understand the energy demand and greenhouse gas emissions from the produced bioenergy, the life cycle assessment technique was implemented. In recent years, several manuscripts have highlighted various advantages and disadvantages of life cycle assessment of algal bioenergy (Chowdhury et al., 2012; Batan et al., 2010; Bradley et al., 2015; Thomassen et al., 2016). Some of these algal bioenergy studies

used dairy manure as a nutrient source for reducing the environmental burden associated with nutrient production (Chowdhury and Freire, 2015; Zhang et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2013). Cumulative energy re- quirements and GHG emissions per unit of bioenergy produced using virgin nutrients or using dairy nutrients depend on the various pro- cesses used to recover energy from the residual biomass left after bio- diesel production. Each process required to produce algal biofuel, ranging from algal biomass production, biodiesel production, algal re- sidue management through pyrolysis, anaerobic digestion, and enzy- matic hydrolysis, need different amount of energy to produce a unit amount of bioenergy. Associated GHG emission for one unit of bioe- nergy production was also varied accordingly (Chowdhury and Freire, 2015). These processes also require considerable time to complete. Hence, if one considers above mentioned processes for bioenergy pro- duction, a considerable time will be required to complete all the pro- cesses considered in a scenario. The major time however, was con- sumed by anaerobic digestion (15-30 and algal biomass production (one to several weeks). Therefore, depending on the pro- cesses included in the scenario and variability in duration of anaerobic digestion and algal biomass production, a run (defined in this study as residence time) for a scenario can last from two weeks to several weeks. Therefore, depending on the residence time in a year, the number of cycles executed will also vary. Depending on the number of cycles executed, bioenergy production can also be varied for a year. Several studies showed the effect of time and nutrient recycling on biorefineries in the form of bioenergy produced, global warming potential (GWP) and energy consumption (Levasseur et al., 2010, 2012; Draper et al., 2015; Montazeri et al., 2016; Chowdhury and Franchetti, 2017). In real world, the waste biorefinery will be based on several processes, there- fore, executing a scenario require some time to complete.

This study was undertaken to understand the effect of residence time on bioenergy production and greenhouse emission from dairy waste produced from a 4000 head cow farm. To understand the in- tricacy related to running an algal biofuel production facility throughout a year, it is assumed that a dairy farm is situated at Ohio, where year-round bioenergy production cannot be possible because of low temperature during winter. The other dairy farm is situated at Hawaii, where year-round algal biomass production can be possible. Dairy waste contains a high concentration of nutrients, hence, dilution of waste is sometimes required. This study also evaluates the effect of diluted waste treatment using algae on bioenergy production and as- sociated GHG emissions and energy demand using a process-based LCA paradigm.

6: 2.1. Goal and scope definition for LCA study

The life cycle assessment method was used to estimate the energy requirement (GJ/GJ) and greenhouse gas emissions (kg CO2 equivalent) from the produced bioenergy using cradle to gate boundary. The functional unit selected is GJ of bioenergy produced from dairy manure. Mass of dairy manure produced from a 4000 cow dairy firm, for a particular residence time, was the input of nutrients for bioenergy production.

The production of bioenergy also consumes energy. Bioenergy production is generally said to be favorable, if produced bioenergy consumes less energy than that which is produced. There are several indicators which depict energy demand of produced bioenergy and a detailed description of various energy demand indicators are given in Malca et al. (2014). For the net energy ratio (NER), or energy re- quirement (ER) estimation, there is an ambiguity regarding the type of energy that is used to estimate these indicators (Azadi et al., 2014; Slade and Bauen, 2013). Arvidsson et al. (2012) reported that some of the data pertaining to energy demand of palm oil production used primary and final energy for estimation of energy demand indicators. In
this study, ER was estimated by using total primary energy require- ment, which also includes primary renewable energy requirement too.

Energy requirement is defined by a ratio [ER] (GJ/GJ) of primary energy demand to the chemical energy produced in the form of bioe- nergy. For estimation of energy requirement (ER), the low heating value was used where appropriate. Greenhouse gas intensity, which is a ratio of greenhouse gas produced (CO2 eq.) from per GJ bioenergy produced is used as an indicator for the greenhouse gas emissions im- pact category. GHG emissions are again divided into energy related, non-energy related and CO2 sequestration. For GHG intensity, the global warming potential for 100 years was used and taken from the pertinent IPCC guidelines (Chowdhury and Freire, 2015). Application of dairy manure without any treatment to the land was used as a re- ference flow.

8: <header>2.1.1. Scenario description </header>

A scenario was constructed using (i) anaerobic digestion of dairy manure, (ii) algal biomass production using effluent from anaerobic digester, (iii) algal biodiesel production, (iv) anaerobic digestion of algal residue remained after oil extraction, and recycling of nutrient- rich effluent to the algal pond, (v) enzymatic hydrolysis of sludge generated from anaerobic digesters, (vi) pyrolysis of enzymatically hydrolyzed residue, and (vii) uses of hydrolyzate for algal biomass and biodiesel production (Fig. 1). A system boundary for the present LCA study is also shown in the Fig. 1. The above mentioned processes are included in the system boundary. Dairy manure is the inflow in the system and bioenergy, emissions and biochar are the outflow from the system. For modeling the algal biorefinery, all the above-mentioned processes were taken as the foreground system for which data were collected from literature (average and conservative values were used). Background data used in this study are previously reported in Chowdhury and Freire (2015) and Chowdhury et al. (2012). Back- ground data was primarily comprised of electric energy, and for this purpose, the US average energy mix was used. For converting the final energy to the primary energy, appropriate conversion factors were used depending on the sources of energy. For nutrients, data were collected from various literature sources, and these data were modified by using the US average energy mix and associated GHG emissions (Chowdhury and Freire, 2015). Details of foreground data are given later.

For the case of a dairy farm in Ohio, where year round operation of main scenario could not be possible, an alternative scenario was de- veloped, where it was assumed that dairy manure was anaerobically digested and energy was recovered as methane. Through this approach, it was possible to run the bioenergy production throughout the year. For simplification of the analysis, it was assumed that duration of the alternative scenario was the same as the main scenario. A reference case was also developed, where it was assumed that dairy manure was ap- plied as raw as a soil amendment. Nutrients present in the dairy manure are in the organic form and uptake of these nutrients varies with plant species, soil type and presence of other soil amendments (Petersen et al., 2003; Svoboda et al., 2013; Collins et al., 2013). After 100 years, it was assumed that 10% of the applied carbon would be sequestered (Bruun et al., 2006). However, for biochar corresponding sequestered carbon was 19% of the applied carbon present in the biochar.

2.1.1.1. Anaerobic digestion (AD). An 80% removal efficiency of COD, 0.25 biogas produced/kg total solid and 60% of the biogas as methane were assumed to model the anaerobic digestion (AD) for dairy waste treatment. AD for residual algal biomass was modeled using data given in Chowdhury and Freire (2015). 65% of the incoming solid bound nutrients was calculated to be solubilized corresponding to 80% COD removal efficiency (Chowdhury et al., 2012).

For the estimation of energy consumption for the AD, the energy content of methane, the fugitive greenhouse gas emissions from the AD were collected from Chowdhury and Freire (2015). Nutrient-rich ef- fluent from the AD was separated from the solid-liquid mixture through

(2015) reported that most of the LCA studies on algal biofuel overestimated the methane production from the AD. Methane production should be below 0.8 m³ /kg of biomass as re- ported by the Collet et al. (2015). Therefore, methane production used in this simulation was below the maximum value and within the range reported by Collet et al. (2015). 
2.1.1.2. Algal growth and subsequent biodiesel production. Nutrient-rich effluent from the AD was pumped to the open raceway pond which has a depth in between 0.5 and 1 m and nitrogen concentration of 250 mg/ L. The nitrogen concentration was assumed to be that of the concentration of diluted dairy waste reported in the literature (Wang et al., 2010; Lu et al., 2015; Hena et al., 2015). The energy estimates for the pumping and mixing, greenhouse gas emissions from the algal pond were collected from the following sources: Sturm and Lamer (2011), 
Fagerstone et al. (2011), and Ferron et al. (2012). A maturation time of 5 to 15 days was assumed for the algal growth. Harvesting (40% solid content) was performed through the centrifugation. Harvested biomass was dried by heat derived from biogas or natural gas. Biodiesel production from the dried biomass was modeled using the methodology and data given in Chowdhury et al. (2012). It was assumed that algae were grown in a batch system, in which nitrogen deprivation occurred in the later stage of the growth phase, and nitrogen deprivation triggered the lipid production in the algal cells. 
2.1.1.3. Enzymatic hydrolysis of biomass. A 35% of the incoming nutrients in an anaerobic digester ended up in the digested sludge. Bound nutrients in the digested sludge can be solubilized using enzymatic hydrolysis and be used for algal growth. Pertinent data for enzymatic hydrolysis was taken from Zheng et al. (2012). 2.1.1.4. Pyrolysis. Pyrolysis is a thermochemical treatment of biomass at high temperature in the absence of oxygen. At high temperature, cracking of biomass occurs and bio-oil, biochar, and non-condensable gases are produced. LCA studies of pyrolysis either calculated energy from various equations or used reported energy derived from experimental condition. 16: In the LCA studies where several products are being produced, al- location of the burden to the products is difficult. When a multiple products are produced, allocation of the burden through mass, eco- nomic value or energy value will not suffice the purpose. In this study, bioenergy in the form of biofuel, biodiesel and biogas are produced. Besides bioenergy, nutrients, in the form of sludge and biochars are also produced. However, as our main concern is to produce bioenergy and incorporating nutrients for allocation requires mass or economic allo- cation, which cannot be applied along with bioenergy. Hence, the system expansion approach was taken into account for waste nutrients. Generally, the system expansion approach was used to estimate the burden (energy and environmental burdens) of conventional products and these burdens were subtracted from the total burden estimated from the production of new products. In this study, nutrients in the form of nitrogen and phosphorus are produced from AD, which are then used for algal growth or soil amendment instead of conventional in- organic nutrients. Biochar was also produced. Biochar has a variable nutrient value and nutrients tend to leach very slowly from biochar. Generally, biochar has been used as a soil amendment which can im- prove soil quality. Therefore, nutrient values of biochar were ignored and no substitution of virgin nutrients was carried out. In the alter- native scenario, which was executed when main scenario could not be executed because of ambient temperature was not conducive for algal growth; soluble nutrients in the form of anaerobically digested effluent and sludge bound nutrients were produced. Generally, availability of soluble nutrients can substitute 100% of the inorganic nutrients. However, substitution of inorganic nutrients by sludge bound nutrients is limited and a 40% substitution for nitrogen and 100% substitution of phosphorus can be taken as a representative value (Hansen et al., 2006).

In addition to nutrients, biogas used to satisfy the internal heat energy demand. Uses of biogas could reduce or eliminate the need of natural gas for supplying heat energy. Therefore, savings of GHG emissions due to replacement of natural gas by biogas was taken into account. GHG emissions in the form of CO2, N20, and CH4 were emitted from uses of fossil fuel from various processes for biofuel production and from the algal pond and from the land applied residual biomass. Besides these sources, GHG emission was also occurred during anae- robic digestion due to escape of methane through the leaky joints of the anaerobic digester. In this study, GHG emission was reported when no avoided burden procedure was included and for the other one, GHG emission was estimated taking into account avoided burden approach.

The average inputs of nutrients are constant for each of the re- sidence time. However, due to internal recycling, the mass of nutrients were increased with progress of the operation (Fig. 1). The average input of nutrients was also increased with increments in residence time (Table 1). Nonrenewable primary energy requirement, which includes fossil fuels from coal, natural gas, petroleum and nuclear power de- creases with an increase in residence time. The non-renewable primary energy requirement was lower for dairy farms located in Ohio when compared to those in Hawaii, because of running of alternative scenario in Ohio, which has lower energy requirement as compared to main scenario. Natural gas was also required for drying algal biomass and a part of the requirement was satisfied by the internal production of biogas from anaerobic digester. For example, for dairy farms in Ohio, excess biogas was produced as compared to requirement for drying (Table 1). Changes in biodiesel production and sequestered carbon production are discussed in details later.

bioenergy could be produced respectively (Table 2). The amount of bioenergy produced in a dairy farm located in Hawaii increased linearly with time, whereas for those located in Ohio, bioenergy production increased until the end of running of the main scenario. For the alter- native scenario, which was executed for part of the year in Ohio, bioenergy production attained a constant value (2.24 X 10³ GJ) per cycle (Fig. 2).

For dairy farms located in Ohio, for 45 and 60 day residence times, bioenergy productions were 59% and 65% respectively, of corre- sponding bioenergy production in Hawaii. Produced bioenergy in Hawaii was mostly comprised of biodiesel (~80%) and rest of the bioenergy was produced through pyrolysis. However, in dairy farms in Ohio, biodiesel contributed ~60% of bioenergy, and another ~20% of the bioenergy was produced from pyrolysis, and rest of the bioenergy was comprised of biogas. Therefore, for those dairy farms located in Ohio, a part of the bioenergy was produced as biogas as the alternative scenario contributed biogas as bioenergy and this scenario was exe- cuted for several months of a year in Ohio. However, in the case of dairy farms in Hawaii, all of the biogas produced through the main scenario, which could be executed throughout the year, consumed internally and could not contribute for bioenergy production. Producing higher amounts of biogas certainly contributes to the attractiveness of the particular scenario. Generally, in the US, petroleum and natural gas are imported. Petroleum is more attractive because of its higher price as compared to natural gas. However, storage of biodiesel is an issue, and current technological know-how could not enable storage of biodiesel for long periods of time. In contrast, biogas can be seamlessly integrated to present storage and combustion technology (Sadhukhan, 2014). Hence it can be said that natural gas can be a more attractive product for near future, whereas biodiesel will be a more attractive product than natural gas when storage for long term will be available.

28: A review of ER obtained from this analysis (Table 3) revealed that, for both the dairy farm locations, the energy requirement increased with an increase in residence time. Little differences in ER were ob- served due to differences in efficiency of the energy that was used for execution of various processes required for production of bioenergy. An increase in residence time from 30 days to 60 days increased the average ER for each state, but the range of ER was almost the same for each of the state, irrespective of the residence time. Reduced ER in the with allocation scenario was due to subtraction of avoided energy generated from the application of nutrients in the form of sludge and residual biomass generated from the algal biorefinery. The decrease in ER in Ohio dairy farms was due to execution of the alternative scenario, in which soluble nutrients produced from anaerobic digestion was ap- plied as the substitute of inorganic nutrients. However, such avoidance of nutrient could not be possible in a dairy farm located in Hawaii, as the main scenario was executed all over the year at Hawaii, and biochar was produced as the end product, and no substitution of nutrient for biochar was considered in this study. Hence, energy requirement for with allocation scenario was lower in a dairy farm in Ohio as compared to Hawaii. Geographical location, year round operation VS running main and alternative scenario did not show much effect on ER. Contrary to this study, Chowdhury and Freire (2015) showed that year round operation VS intermittent operation could affect the energy requirement and bioenergy production. The difference in results was due to in- corporating all the states of the USA in the bioenergy calculation and in most of the states bioenergy production could only be done for a part of year. Contrary to the present study, no such alternative scenario was incorporated to run the bioenergy production throughout the year. Several other studies also estimated the energy demand for various pathways of bioenergy production, using algae as a feedstock. Slade and

Bauen (2013) reviewed some of the LCA based bioenergy studies. They demonstrated that most of the raceway based bioenergy production through algal route have shown ER below one, whereas, photo- bioreactor (PBR) based algae growth system showed ER higher than one. Contrary to the above mentioned study, Lopez et al. (2017) re- ported that pilot scale PBR has a better energy return as compared to the open raceway pond especially for the climatic condition prevalent in the Netherlands. However, these studies used only algae production and biodiesel production from the algal biomass. Incorporating residual biomass utilization for further value added products formation would reduce the energy demand (Chowdhury et al., 2012, Chowdhury and Freire, 2015). Pilot scale studies on wastewater treatment through the raceway ponds, utilization of the algal biomass for biofuel production, and residual biomass management through anaerobic digestion have been demonstrated. These studies showed that incorporating AD re- duced the energy demand of the produced biofuel (Joshi and Wang, 2018; Lijo et al., 2018).

Biofuel production through stand-alone thermochemical treatment i.e. hydrothermal liquefaction and pyrolysis of algal biomass has shown an ER greater than one. Especially, pyrolysis procedure consumed more energy than that it produced (ER: 2.27). Contrary to the pyrolysis procedure, hydrothermal liquefaction showed improved ER (1.23). Increased ER for pyrolysis process was due to energy requirement for the drying of algal biomass (Bennion et al., 2015). As compared to Bennion et al. (2015), Chowdhury and Freire (2015) reported that in- corporating the pyrolysis process for residual biomass management along with other processes make the overall bioenergy production en- ergy positive (produce more energy as compared to energy consump- tion), although the ER of a standalone pyrolysis process was greater than one.

31: GHG emissions were also divided into two parts (i) GHG emission due to energy consumption and (ii) GHG emissions generated due to non-energy related activities such as GHG emissions from land appli- cation, emission from anaerobic digester and from algal ponds. Total GHG emissions per GJ bioenergy produced for Ohio and Hawaii are tabulated in Table 4. For without allocation scenario, the average GHG emissions were within 21-38 kg CO2 eq./GJ of bioenergy produced. For both the states, for a particular residence time, GHG emissions were lower in Hawaii as compared to Ohio (varies within 3-4 kg CO2/GJ bioenergy produced). An increase in residence time (30 days to 60 days) showed little effect on the GHG emission, i.e. an increase in residence time decreases the GHG emission by 1 and 2kg CO2/GJ bioenergy
32: GHG emission from algal bioenergy production number in the first row are the GHG emission for 250 mg/L and for 100 mg/L N concentration GHG emissions are shown in second row. Number in brackets showed the weighted average GHG emission.

produced. Therefore, it seems that higher bioenergy production in Hawaii does not increase the GHG emission per unit of bioenergy produced. Therefore, running the main scenario over the whole year is preferred, as compared to running the alternative scenario, if weather could permit. The lowest GHG emissions was observed for Hawaii (28 kg CO2/GJ bioenergy) at 60 days residence time and this value was increased to 32 kg CO2/GJ bioenergy produced at 30 days residence time. For Ohio, at 60 and 30 days residence time, the lowest GHG emissions were 32 and 35 kg CO2/GJ bioenergy produced respectively. As compared to the without allocation scenario, GHG emissions re- duction was observed for with allocation scenario (Table 4). In the 'with allocation' scenario, GHG emissions reduction was due to production of biogas which was used to satisfy the internal energy demand and for reduction in CO2 emission, which would be produced from burning natural gas. Except substitution of GHG emissions from natural gas production, substitution of nutrients in the form of applied sludge on the land also saves GHG emissions, which can be produced from pro- duction of substituted inorganic nutrients during running of the alter- native scenario.

Energy related GHG emission increased incrementally with re- sidence time (per GJ energy produced) for both the states. Similar to the energy-related GHG emission, non-energy related GHG emissions were also higher in the dairy farms that are located in Ohio as compared to Hawaii. For non-energy related GHG emission, emission occurs from various sources such as anaerobic digester, algal ponds and from the land application of biomass. In Ohio, the higher amount of non-energy related GHG emission occurred due to increased amount of biomass was applied on the land. In Ohio, most part of the year alternative scenario was executed, and produced more biomass in the form of sludge from AD for land application and produced less bioenergy. For this reason, the non-energy related GHG emission appears higher in Ohio than that in Hawaii (Table 5). Energy related GHG emissions also have special implication for greening or reducing the GHG emission from the pro- duced biofuel. Introducing renewable energy dominated energy mix for bioenergy production would reduce the GHG emissions of produced biofuel (Ayer and Dias, 2018).

Another important observation from the Table 5 is that there is little difference in CO2 sequestration from land-applied biomass for both the states. CO2 sequestration was lower for a dairy farm in Hawaii (34-41 CO2/GJ bioenergy produced) as compared to one located in Ohio (35-42 kg CO2/GJ bioenergy produced). Although, more residual biomass was produced in Ohio, which was applied on the land, residual biomass produced in Hawaii was formed as biochar and higher carbon sequestration could be achieved from biochar (19% of the applied carbon), as compared to land applied sludge or biomass (10% of the applied carbon). Therefore, even though, a higher amount of biomass was applied in Ohio, an increase in the form of sequestered carbon was not observed. There was a decrease in nutrient concentration from 250 mg/L to 100 mg/L however, the average GHG emission increased by at least 5 kg CO2/GJ bioenergy produced. Similar to the 250 mg/L N concentration, at 100 mg/L N concentration, an increase in residence time decreased GHG emissions by 1 kg CO2/GJ bioenergy produced. For the 'with allocation" scenario, at Ohio, the average GHG emissions were 4, 1 and CO2/GJ bioenergy produced for 30, 45 and 60 day re- sidence time respectively. Corresponding values of GHG emissions (kg CO2/GJ bioenergy) in Hawaii were 2, and -4. Therefore, for both the locations, GHG emission were increased by at least 9 kg CO2/GJ of bioenergy produced due to decrease in N content from 250 mg/L to 100 mg/L.

Average GHG emissions, energy related GHG emission, non-energy related GHG emission and CO2 sequestration were also increased due to the decrease in nitrogen concentration (from 250 mg/L to 100 mg/L). There are numerous studies that have reported energy demand and GHG emission from bioenergy produced from various feedstock ranging from algae, forest residues, woody biomass etc. (Chowdhury and Freire, 2015; Sadhukhan and Hernandez, 2017; Sanwal et al., 2017). Bennion et al. (2015) reported that hydrothermal liquefaction of algal biomass ultimately sequestered CO2 (-11kg CO2/GJ bioenergy produced), whereas pyrolysis based bioenergy production showed GHG emissions within 210 kg CO2eq./GJ bioenergy. Incorporating several upstream and downstream process before and after of hydrothermal or pyrolysis procedure could drastically reduce the GHG emission of the produced bioenergy. For example, Nie and Bi (2018) reported that GHG emissions from the integrated biorefinery varied between 17 and 20 kg CO2 eq./ GJ bioenergy produced. Reported energy demands and GHG emissions also vary depending on the chosen system boundaries, products pro- duced and allocation procedure used (Malca et al., 2014; Chowdhury et al., 2012). In the proposed algal biorefineries, it is most likely that some of the processes will run in a batch mode, for example, thermo- chemical treatment of biomass (pyrolysis, hydrothermal liquefaction) enzymatic hydrolysis of biomass. With the exception of biomass pro- cessing and pretreatment, most of the reported studies on algal biomass production in ponds and photobioreactors have been done in batch scale. Therefore, it is most likely that even in the near future some of the processes in proposed algal biorefineries will run in batch mode and for completion of one cycle in a biorefinery will consume some time. In later stages, when most of the processes would be run in continuous mode, hydraulic residence time (HRT) can be varied depending on the HRT of each process. Therefore, this study is the first to estimate the effect of residence time on energy demand and GHG emission from bioenergy production. Complementary to the present study, Guieysse et al. (2013) performed a sensitivity analysis to understand the effect of various parameters on water demand of algal biofuel. They reported that residence time is the second most sensitive parameter for water demand generated from algal bioenergy production. This study showed that increase in residence time, decreased the bioenergy production. For example, if one considers the bioenergy production in a dairy farm located in Hawaii, increasing the residence time from 30 days to 45 days decreased the bioenergy production by 22%. Increasing the residence time from 45 days to 60 days, however, decreased the bioe- nergy production by 16%. The time vs bioenergy production showed a nonlinear behavior. To elucidate this behavior in a much better fashion, bioenergy productions for 20 and 70 days residence time were also estimated (Fig. 3). As shown in the Fig. 3, bioenergy production de- creased with increase in residence time by a power law. If one tries to estimate the slope of the curve (difference in bioenergy production/ difference in residence time), it was observed that the slope has a higher value in lower residence time as compared to higher residence time. This means that decrease in energy production is likely more to the change of residence time from 20 days to 30 days as compared to 60 days to 70 days. Montazeri et al. (2016) reported that time is also important for producing desired products from a biorefinery. De- pending on the time of harvest, algae can accumulate more proteins than carbohydrates or lipids. Hence, the completion time of a cycle in a biorefinery has an influence on energy production and its associated GHG emission and energy demand. In the proposed biorefinery, most of

the time was consumed by the anaerobic digestion process. HRT of the anaerobic digesters treating dairy waste or algae have shown to vary between 15 and 30 days (Damirer and Chen, 2005; Atandi and Rahman, 2012). AD is an old technology and has been the subject of extensive previous research. Hence, there is little likelihood for drastic reductions in residence times for an anaerobic digestion process. AD also does not produce any products which have high economic value. Electricity generated from natural gas that was derived from AD was found to cost more than the average cost of electricity production (Atandi and Rahman, 2012). However, for dairy waste based biorefinery, AD is an indispensable technology. AD has been implemented in the US to re- duce GHG emissions from the storing of dairy manure in otherwise cheap anaerobic lagoons. During storage of manure, a considerable amount GHG emission takes place (Wang et al., 2018). AD also helps to solubilize the nutrients which are being used for algal biomass pro- duction. Produced biogas can reduce the need of external energy source for drying algal biomass which is the most energy intensive process in algal biofuel production (Chowdhury et al., 2012; Sander and Murthy, 2010). HRT of an AD can be reduced if the digester is operated in the thermophilic condition. However, maintaining thermophilic conditions requires additional energy for heating, and thermophilic reactors showed less stability when compared to mesophilic reactors. In an algal biorefinery, algal biomass is the main raw material, which contains carbohydrates, lipids and proteins. Lipids and carbohydrates are the main ingredients for producing biofuels. In the generation of algal biomass, lipids or carbohydrates are being accumulated during nitrogen deficient conditions. Recently, several studies showed algae-based municipal wastewater treatment with an HRT of days, however, attainment of 1-2 days HRT is most likely not possible for high strength wastewater i.e. anaerobically digested dairy manure. Most of the stu- dies on algae based treatment of anaerobically digested dairy manure (raw or diluted) reported an HRT between 7 and 14 days. Moreover, producing value-added products in the form of lipids and carbohydrates

could not be produced within 7 days, as these products are being pro- duced during nitrogen deficient conditions (Wang et al., 2010; Lu et al., 2015). Other than AD and algal pond, other processes included in this study i.e. pyrolysis and enzymatic hydrolysis occur very quickly and residence times varied between a few minutes to hours. Hence, there will be little scope to reduce the retention time beyond the values as- sumed in this study. Incorporating pyrolysis provides production of biofuel which can be upgraded to gasoline and diesel. Hence, pyrolysis can provide avenues for producing high value products from biomass within a short residence time.

As compared to energy production, estimated GHG emissions were within the range reported by other waste nutrient based bioenergy production. Recently, Fortier et al. (2014) reported that GHG emissions from hydrothermal liquefaction based bioenergy production using algal biomass showed GHG emission ranging from 35.2-86.5 kg CO2/GJ bioenergy produced. Souza et al. (2015) analyzed algal biofuel pro- duction integrated with a sugarcane biorefinery. They observed that GHG emission from the algal biofuel production (per GJ bioenergy produced) was within 14-33 kg CO2 with a mean of 23 kg CO2. The reported GHG emission by Souza et al. (2015) was lower than the present study and one of the main reasons behind the increase in bioenergy production was due to increase in extent of biomass pro- cessing as reported by Chowdhury and Freire (2015). Moreover, for GHG emission, Souza et al. (2015) reported that non-energy related GHG emission was estimated to be 27% of the total GHG emission as they estimated, whereas non-energy related GHG emission in this study was estimated as about 32%.

produced. Therefore, it seems that higher bioenergy production in Hawaii does not increase the GHG emission per unit of bioenergy produced. Therefore, running the main scenario over the whole year is preferred, as compared to running the alternative scenario, if weather could permit. The lowest GHG emissions was observed for Hawaii (28 kg CO2/GJ bioenergy) at 60 days residence time and this value was increased to 32 kg CO2/GJ bioenergy produced at 30 days residence time. For Ohio, at 60 and 30 days residence time, the lowest GHG emissions were 32 and 35 kg CO2/GJ bioenergy produced respectively. As compared to the without allocation scenario, GHG emissions re- duction was observed for with allocation scenario (Table 4). In the 'with allocation' scenario, GHG emissions reduction was due to production of biogas which was used to satisfy the internal energy demand and for reduction in CO2 emission, which would be produced from burning natural gas. Except substitution of GHG emissions from natural gas production, substitution of nutrients in the form of applied sludge on the land also saves GHG emissions, which can be produced from pro- duction of substituted inorganic nutrients during running of the alter- native scenario.

Energy related GHG emission increased incrementally with re- sidence time (per GJ energy produced) for both the states. Similar to the energy-related GHG emission, non-energy related GHG emissions were also higher in the dairy farms that are located in Ohio as compared to Hawaii. For non-energy related GHG emission, emission occurs from various sources such as anaerobic digester, algal ponds and from the land application of biomass. In Ohio, the higher amount of non-energy related GHG emission occurred due to increased amount of biomass was applied on the land. In Ohio, most part of the year alternative scenario was executed, and produced more biomass in the form of sludge from AD for land application and produced less bioenergy. For this reason, the non-energy related GHG emission appears higher in Ohio than that in Hawaii (Table 5). Energy related GHG emissions also have special implication for greening or reducing the GHG emission from the pro- duced biofuel. Introducing renewable energy dominated energy mix for bioenergy production would reduce the GHG emissions of produced biofuel (Ayer and Dias, 2018).

Another important observation from the Table 5 is that there is little difference in CO2 sequestration from land-applied biomass for both the states. CO2 sequestration was lower for a dairy farm in Hawaii (34-41 CO2/GJ bioenergy produced) as compared to one located in Ohio (35-42 kg CO2/GJ bioenergy produced). Although, more residual biomass was produced in Ohio, which was applied on the land, residual biomass produced in Hawaii was formed as biochar and higher carbon sequestration could be achieved from biochar (19% of the applied carbon), as compared to land applied sludge or biomass (10% of the applied carbon). Therefore, even though, a higher amount of biomass was applied in Ohio, an increase in the form of sequestered carbon was not observed. There was a decrease in nutrient concentration from 250 mg/L to 100 mg/L however, the average GHG emission increased by at least 5 kg CO2/GJ bioenergy produced. Similar to the 250 mg/L N concentration, at 100 mg/L N concentration, an increase in residence time decreased GHG emissions by 1 kg CO2/GJ bioenergy produced. For the 'with allocation" scenario, at Ohio, the average GHG emissions were 4, 1 and CO2/GJ bioenergy produced for 30, 45 and 60 day re- sidence time respectively. Corresponding values of GHG emissions (kg CO2/GJ bioenergy) in Hawaii were 2, and -4. Therefore, for both the locations, GHG emission were increased by at least 9 kg CO2/GJ of bioenergy produced due to decrease in N content from 250 mg/L to 100 mg/L.

Average GHG emissions, energy related GHG emission, non-energy related GHG emission and CO2 sequestration were also increased due to the decrease in nitrogen concentration (from 250 mg/L to 100 mg/L). There are numerous studies that have reported energy demand and GHG emission from bioenergy produced from various feedstock ranging from algae, forest residues, woody biomass etc. (Chowdhury and Freire, 2015; Sadhukhan and Hernandez, 2017; Sanwal et al., 2017). Bennion et al. (2015) reported that hydrothermal liquefaction of algal biomass ultimately sequestered CO2 (-11kg CO2/GJ bioenergy produced), whereas pyrolysis based bioenergy production showed GHG emissions within 210 kg CO2eq./GJ bioenergy. Incorporating several upstream and downstream process before and after of hydrothermal or pyrolysis procedure could drastically reduce the GHG emission of the produced bioenergy. For example, Nie and Bi (2018) reported that GHG emissions from the integrated biorefinery varied between 17 and 20 kg CO2 eq./ GJ bioenergy produced. Reported energy demands and GHG emissions also vary depending on the chosen system boundaries, products pro- duced and allocation procedure used (Malca et al., 2014; Chowdhury et al., 2012). In the proposed algal biorefineries, it is most likely that some of the processes will run in a batch mode, for example, thermo- chemical treatment of biomass (pyrolysis, hydrothermal liquefaction) enzymatic hydrolysis of biomass. With the exception of biomass pro- cessing and pretreatment, most of the reported studies on algal biomass production in ponds and photobioreactors have been done in batch scale. Therefore, it is most likely that even in the near future some of the processes in proposed algal biorefineries will run in batch mode and for completion of one cycle in a biorefinery will consume some time. In later stages, when most of the processes would be run in continuous mode, hydraulic residence time (HRT) can be varied depending on the HRT of each process. Therefore, this study is the first to estimate the effect of residence time on energy demand and GHG emission from bioenergy production. Complementary to the present study, Guieysse et al. (2013) performed a sensitivity analysis to understand the effect of various parameters on water demand of algal biofuel. They reported that residence time is the second most sensitive parameter for water demand generated from algal bioenergy production. This study showed that increase in residence time, decreased the bioenergy production. For example, if one considers the bioenergy production in a dairy farm located in Hawaii, increasing the residence time from 30 days to 45 days decreased the bioenergy production by 22%. Increasing the residence time from 45 days to 60 days, however, decreased the bioe- nergy production by 16%. The time vs bioenergy production showed a nonlinear behavior. To elucidate this behavior in a much better fashion, bioenergy productions for 20 and 70 days residence time were also estimated (Fig. 3). As shown in the Fig. 3, bioenergy production de- creased with increase in residence time by a power law. If one tries to estimate the slope of the curve (difference in bioenergy production/ difference in residence time), it was observed that the slope has a higher value in lower residence time as compared to higher residence time. This means that decrease in energy production is likely more to the change of residence time from 20 days to 30 days as compared to 60 days to 70 days. Montazeri et al. (2016) reported that time is also important for producing desired products from a biorefinery. De- pending on the time of harvest, algae can accumulate more proteins than carbohydrates or lipids. Hence, the completion time of a cycle in a biorefinery has an influence on energy production and its associated GHG emission and energy demand. In the proposed biorefinery, most of
Fig. 3. Bioenergy produced (GJ/year) for residence time ranging from 20 to 70 days.

the time was consumed by the anaerobic digestion process. HRT of the anaerobic digesters treating dairy waste or algae have shown to vary between 15 and 30 days (Damirer and Chen, 2005; Atandi and Rahman, 2012). AD is an old technology and has been the subject of extensive previous research. Hence, there is little likelihood for drastic reductions in residence times for an anaerobic digestion process. AD also does not produce any products which have high economic value. Electricity generated from natural gas that was derived from AD was found to cost more than the average cost of electricity production (Atandi and Rahman, 2012). However, for dairy waste based biorefinery, AD is an indispensable technology. AD has been implemented in the US to re- duce GHG emissions from the storing of dairy manure in otherwise cheap anaerobic lagoons. During storage of manure, a considerable amount GHG emission takes place (Wang et al., 2018). AD also helps to solubilize the nutrients which are being used for algal biomass pro- duction. Produced biogas can reduce the need of external energy source for drying algal biomass which is the most energy intensive process in algal biofuel production (Chowdhury et al., 2012; Sander and Murthy, 2010). HRT of an AD can be reduced if the digester is operated in the thermophilic condition. However, maintaining thermophilic conditions requires additional energy for heating, and thermophilic reactors showed less stability when compared to mesophilic reactors. In an algal biorefinery, algal biomass is the main raw material, which contains carbohydrates, lipids and proteins. Lipids and carbohydrates are the main ingredients for producing biofuels. In the generation of algal biomass, lipids or carbohydrates are being accumulated during nitrogen deficient conditions. Recently, several studies showed algae-based municipal wastewater treatment with an HRT of days, however, attainment of 1-2 days HRT is most likely not possible for high strength wastewater i.e. anaerobically digested dairy manure. Most of the stu- dies on algae based treatment of anaerobically digested dairy manure (raw or diluted) reported an HRT between 7 and 14 days. Moreover, producing value-added products in the form of lipids and carbohydrates

could not be produced within 7 days, as these products are being pro- duced during nitrogen deficient conditions (Wang et al., 2010; Lu et al., 2015). Other than AD and algal pond, other processes included in this study i.e. pyrolysis and enzymatic hydrolysis occur very quickly and residence times varied between a few minutes to hours. Hence, there will be little scope to reduce the retention time beyond the values as- sumed in this study. Incorporating pyrolysis provides production of biofuel which can be upgraded to gasoline and diesel. Several studies also showed that pyrolysis can be used to produce jet fuels and other compounds of high economic values. Hence, pyrolysis can provide avenues for producing high value products from biomass within a short residence time.

As compared to energy production, estimated GHG emissions were within the range reported by other waste nutrient based bioenergy production. Recently, Fortier et al. (2014) reported that GHG emissions from hydrothermal liquefaction based bioenergy production using algal biomass showed GHG emission ranging from 35.2-86.5 kg CO2/GJ bioenergy produced. Souza et al. (2015) analyzed algal biofuel pro- duction integrated with a sugarcane biorefinery. They observed that GHG emission from the algal biofuel production (per GJ bioenergy produced) was within 14-33 kg CO2 with a mean of 23 kg CO2. The reported GHG emission by Souza et al. (2015) was lower than the present study and one of the main reasons behind the increase in bioenergy production was due to increase in extent of biomass pro- cessing as reported by Chowdhury and Freire (2015). Moreover, for GHG emission, Souza et al. (2015) reported that non-energy related GHG emission was estimated to be 27% of the total GHG emission as they estimated, whereas non-energy related GHG emission in this study was estimated as about 32%.

In Ohio, most part of the year alternative scenario was executed, and produced more biomass in the form of sludge from AD for land application and produced less bioenergy. For this reason, the non-energy related GHG emission appears higher in Ohio than that in Hawaii (Table 5). Energy related GHG emissions also have special implication for greening or reducing the GHG emission from the pro- duced biofuel. Introducing renewable energy dominated energy mix for bioenergy production would reduce the GHG emissions of produced biofuel (Ayer and Dias, 2018).

produced. Therefore, it seems that higher bioenergy production in Hawaii does not increase the GHG emission per unit of bioenergy produced. For this reason, the non-energy related GHG emission appears higher in Ohio than that in Hawaii (Table 5). There are numerous studies that have reported energy demand and GHG emission from bioenergy produced from various feedstock ranging from algae, forest residues, woody biomass etc. (Chowdhury and Freire, 2015; Sadhukhan and Hernandez, 2017; Sanwal et al., 2017). Bennion et al. (2015) reported that hydrothermal liquefaction of algal biomass ultimately sequestered CO2 (-11kg CO2/GJ bioenergy produced), whereas pyrolysis based bioenergy production showed GHG emissions within 210 kg CO2eq./GJ bioenergy. Incorporating several upstream and downstream process before and after of hydrothermal or pyrolysis procedure could drastically reduce the GHG emission of the produced bioenergy. For example, Nie and Bi (2018) reported that GHG emissions from the integrated biorefinery varied between 17 and 20 kg CO2 eq./ GJ bioenergy produced. Reported energy demands and GHG emissions also vary depending on the chosen system boundaries, products pro- duced and allocation procedure used (Malca et al., 2014; Chowdhury et al., 2012). In the proposed algal biorefineries, it is most likely that some of the processes will run in a batch mode, for example, thermo- chemical treatment of biomass (pyrolysis, hydrothermal liquefaction) enzymatic hydrolysis of biomass. With the exception of biomass pro- cessing and pretreatment, most of the reported studies on algal biomass production in ponds and photobioreactors have been done in batch scale. Therefore, it is most likely that even in the near future some of the processes in proposed algal biorefineries will run in batch mode and for completion of one cycle in a biorefinery will consume some time. In later stages, when most of the processes would be run in continuous mode, hydraulic residence time (HRT) can be varied depending on the HRT of each process. Therefore, this study is the first to estimate the effect of residence time on energy demand and GHG emission from bioenergy production. Complementary to the present study, Guieysse et al. (2013) performed a sensitivity analysis to understand the effect of various parameters on water demand of algal biofuel. They reported that residence time is the second most sensitive parameter for water demand generated from algal bioenergy production. This study showed that increase in residence time, decreased the bioenergy production. For example, if one considers the bioenergy production in a dairy farm located in Hawaii, increasing the residence time from 30 days to 45 days decreased the bioenergy production by 22%. Increasing the residence time from 45 days to 60 days, however, decreased the bioe- nergy production by 16%. The time vs bioenergy production showed a nonlinear behavior. To elucidate this behavior in a much better fashion, bioenergy productions for 20 and 70 days residence time were also estimated (Fig. 3). As shown in the Fig. 3, bioenergy production de- creased with increase in residence time by a power law. If one tries to estimate the slope of the curve (difference in bioenergy production/ difference in residence time), it was observed that the slope has a higher value in lower residence time as compared to higher residence time. This means that decrease in energy production is likely more to the change of residence time from 20 days to 30 days as compared to 60 days to 70 days. Montazeri et al. (2016) reported that time is also important for producing desired products from a biorefinery. De- pending on the time of harvest, algae can accumulate more proteins than carbohydrates or lipids. Hence, the completion time of a cycle in a biorefinery has an influence on energy production and its associated GHG emission and energy demand. In the proposed biorefinery, most of
Fig. 3. Bioenergy produced (GJ/year) for residence time ranging from 20 to 70 days.

the time was consumed by the anaerobic digestion process. HRT of the anaerobic digesters treating dairy waste or algae have shown to vary between 15 and 30 days (Damirer and Chen, 2005; Atandi and Rahman, 2012). AD is an old technology and has been the subject of extensive previous research. Hence, there is little likelihood for drastic reductions in residence times for an anaerobic digestion process. AD also does not produce any products which have high economic value. Electricity generated from natural gas that was derived from AD was found to cost more than the average cost of electricity production (Atandi and Rahman, 2012). However, for dairy waste based biorefinery, AD is an indispensable technology. AD has been implemented in the US to re- duce GHG emissions from the storing of dairy manure in otherwise cheap anaerobic lagoons. During storage of manure, a considerable amount GHG emission takes place (Wang et al., 2018). AD also helps to solubilize the nutrients which are being used for algal biomass pro- duction. Produced biogas can reduce the need of external energy source for drying algal biomass which is the most energy intensive process in algal biofuel production (Chowdhury et al., 2012; Sander and Murthy, 2010). HRT of an AD can be reduced if the digester is operated in the thermophilic condition. However, maintaining thermophilic conditions requires additional energy for heating, and thermophilic reactors showed less stability when compared to mesophilic reactors. In an algal biorefinery, algal biomass is the main raw material, which contains carbohydrates, lipids and proteins. Lipids and carbohydrates are the main ingredients for producing biofuels. In the generation of algal biomass, lipids or carbohydrates are being accumulated during nitrogen deficient conditions. Recently, several studies showed algae-based municipal wastewater treatment with an HRT of days, however, attainment of 1-2 days HRT is most likely not possible for high strength wastewater i.e. anaerobically digested dairy manure. Most of the stu- dies on algae based treatment of anaerobically digested dairy manure (raw or diluted) reported an HRT between 7 and 14 days. Moreover, producing value-added products in the form of lipids and carbohydrates

could not be produced within 7 days, as these products are being pro- duced during nitrogen deficient conditions (Wang et al., 2010; Lu et al., 2015). Other than AD and algal pond, other processes included in this study i.e. pyrolysis and enzymatic hydrolysis occur very quickly and residence times varied between a few minutes to hours. Hence, there will be little scope to reduce the retention time beyond the values as- sumed in this study. Incorporating pyrolysis provides production of biofuel which can be upgraded to gasoline and diesel. Several studies also showed that pyrolysis can be used to produce jet fuels and other compounds of high economic values. Hence, pyrolysis can provide avenues for producing high value products from biomass within a short residence time.

the time was consumed by the anaerobic digestion process. HRT of an AD can be reduced if the digester is operated in the thermophilic condition. However, maintaining thermophilic conditions requires additional energy for heating, and thermophilic reactors showed less stability when compared to mesophilic reactors. Moreover, producing value-added products in the form of lipids and carbohydrates

could not be produced within 7 days, as these products are being pro- duced during nitrogen deficient conditions (Wang et al., 2010; Lu et al., 2015). Hence, there will be little scope to reduce the retention time beyond the values as- sumed in this study. (2015) analyzed algal biofuel pro- duction integrated with a sugarcane biorefinery. Except substitution of GHG emissions from natural gas production, substitution of nutrients in the form of applied sludge on the land also saves GHG emissions, which can be produced from pro- duction of substituted inorganic nutrients during running of the alter- native scenario.

Energy related GHG emission increased incrementally with re- sidence time (per GJ energy produced) for both the states. Similar to the energy-related GHG emission, non-energy related GHG emissions were also higher in the dairy farms that are located in Ohio as compared to Hawaii. For non-energy related GHG emission, emission occurs from various sources such as anaerobic digester, algal ponds and from the land application of biomass. In Ohio, the higher amount of non-energy related GHG emission occurred due to increased amount of biomass was applied on the land. In Ohio, most part of the year alternative scenario was executed, and produced more biomass in the form of sludge from AD for land application and produced less bioenergy. For this reason, the non-energy related GHG emission appears higher in Ohio than that in Hawaii (Table 5). Energy related GHG emissions also have special implication for greening or reducing the GHG emission from the pro- duced biofuel. Introducing renewable energy dominated energy mix for bioenergy production would reduce the GHG emissions of produced biofuel (Ayer and Dias, 2018).

There are numerous studies that have reported energy demand and GHG emission from bioenergy produced from various feedstock ranging from algae, forest residues, woody biomass etc. (Chowdhury and Freire, 2015; Sadhukhan and Hernandez, 2017; Sanwal et al., 2017). (2015) reported that hydrothermal liquefaction of algal biomass ultimately sequestered CO2 (-11kg CO2/GJ bioenergy produced), whereas pyrolysis based bioenergy production showed GHG emissions within 210 kg CO2eq./GJ bioenergy. Incorporating several upstream and downstream process before and after of hydrothermal or pyrolysis procedure could drastically reduce the GHG emission of the produced bioenergy. For example, Nie and Bi (2018) reported that GHG emissions from the integrated biorefinery varied between 17 and 20 kg CO2 eq./ GJ bioenergy produced. Reported energy demands and GHG emissions also vary depending on the chosen system boundaries, products pro- duced and allocation procedure used (Malca et al., 2014; Chowdhury et al., 2012). In the proposed algal biorefineries, it is most likely that some of the processes will run in a batch mode, for example, thermo- chemical treatment of biomass (pyrolysis, hydrothermal liquefaction) enzymatic hydrolysis of biomass. With the exception of biomass pro- cessing and pretreatment, most of the reported studies on algal biomass production in ponds and photobioreactors have been done in batch scale. Therefore, it is most likely that even in the near future some of the processes in proposed algal biorefineries will run in batch mode and for completion of one cycle in a biorefinery will consume some time. In later stages, when most of the processes would be run in continuous mode, hydraulic residence time (HRT) can be varied depending on the HRT of each process. Therefore, this study is the first to estimate the effect of residence time on energy demand and GHG emission from bioenergy production. Complementary to the present study, Guieysse et al. (2013) performed a sensitivity analysis to understand the effect of various parameters on water demand of algal biofuel. They reported that residence time is the second most sensitive parameter for water demand generated from algal bioenergy production. This study showed that increase in residence time, decreased the bioenergy production. To elucidate this behavior in a much better fashion, bioenergy productions for 20 and 70 days residence time were also estimated (Fig. 3). Decrease in N content also increased bioenergy production with decreased en- ergy demand (per GJ bioenergy produced).
